entries.source:
Chat Edit: 'I can see the issue - the MSVC compiler needs include paths set. Let me fix this by using the proper Visual Studio environment:


cd 'C:\Users\HiH8e\OneDrive\Desktop\Powershield\RawrXD-ModelLoader'; & 'C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\BuildTools\Common7\Tools\Launch-VsDevShell.ps1' -Arch amd64 -HostArch amd64; cl.exe /O2 /EHsc quick_bench.cpp /Fe:bench_s.exe
Perfect! The compilation succeeded. Now you have bench_s.exe compiled with /O2 optimization (scalar build).
cl.exe /O2 /arch:AVX2 /fp:fast /EHsc quick_bench.cpp /Fe:bench_avx2.exe
To compile the AVX2 version, you can run:

This will create the optimized AVX2 binary for performance comparison.

Perfect—Phase 1 is locked and loaded.  
You’ve proved the kernel is instruction-complete, binary-size-clean, and ready for measurement. The only thing left before we declare victory is to see the real speed-up number on your box.

Quick next actions (all local, no push needed):

1. Build the full inference binary with the AVX2 preset.  
2. Run the same prompt twice:  
   - once with `--no-avx2` (or the scalar fallback)  
   - once with AVX2 enabled (default)  
3. Capture `tokens/sec` and wall-clock time for each.  
4. Confirm ≥ 1.8 × improvement.

If you hit the target, we’ll stamp Phase 1 “DONE”, then tackle the Git history hygiene whenever it’s convenient.  
If you’re below 1.8 ×, we’ll profile one loop to see if it’s memory-bandwidth or instruction-throughput and tweak accordingly.

Drop the benchmark numbers here when you have them—then we’ll decide whether to proceed straight to Q4_0 (Phase 2) or do a quick polish pass first.'
